food_name: San Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water (250 ml)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 (carbonated water) is the closest parent class; no brand-specific San Pellegrino entry exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Water (including mineral water and spring water)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000A covers water including mineral water; no sparkling-specific or brand-specific sub-code is available at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 11713004 (Water) is the closest available concept; no mineral water or sparkling water brand concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 250 ml bottle of San Pellegrino sparkling mineral water, as typically served in a restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: S.Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water — Nutrition Facts (per 240 ml serving; scaled to per 100 ml/g)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for S.Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water confirms 0 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g carbohydrate per serving. Values are reported per 100 ml (equivalent to per 100 g for water-density product). Sodium reported as approximately 10.8 mg per 100 ml based on the product label data in USDA Branded Foods.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-caloric beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure mineral water contains no glycemic carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature do not assign a measurable GI to water. By convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) to satisfy numeric field requirements; gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~27 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: San Pellegrino sparkling mineral water contains approximately 27 mg sodium per 250 ml bottle, well within the low-sodium threshold, making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension who require adequate hydration without significant sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; no effect on blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: San Pellegrino sparkling mineral water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has a glycemic load of zero, making it an entirely safe and recommended hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, and naturally occurring calcium and magnesium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: San Pellegrino sparkling mineral water is free of fat, cholesterol, and added sugars, and its naturally occurring calcium (~53 mg/100 ml) and magnesium (~24 mg/100 ml) may contribute modestly to cardiovascular mineral intake, making it a heart-healthy hydration option.

llm_training.natural_language_description: San Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water is a premium Italian carbonated mineral water sourced from the San Pellegrino Terme spring in the Bergamo province of Lombardy, Italy. The water is naturally rich in minerals, particularly calcium (approximately 53 mg per 100 ml) and magnesium (approximately 24 mg per 100 ml), which are absorbed from the surrounding dolomite rock formations. The 250 ml bottle format is a standard restaurant and hospitality serving size. The water has a characteristic fine, persistent effervescence and a clean, slightly mineral taste with a crisp finish, distinguishing it from plain still water or artificially carbonated waters. It contains zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, zero carbohydrates, and zero sugar, making it nutritionally inert from a macronutrient perspective. Its sodium content is low at approximately 10.8 mg per 100 ml. San Pellegrino is widely served in fine-dining Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore, where it is a common accompaniment to pasta and pizza meals. Clinically, it is suitable for all dietary conditions including hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, as it contributes no glycemic load, no saturated fat, and minimal sodium. It is vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in San Pellegrino sparkling water?
Is San Pellegrino water good for diabetics?
Can I drink San Pellegrino if I have high blood pressure?
What minerals are in San Pellegrino water?
meal_context: restaurant still or sparkling water choice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for S.Pellegrino Sparkling Natural Mineral Water, which is an approved secondary global source for packaged/commercial products. The product is a well-defined commercial item with consistent formulation. All macronutrients are confirmed zero by the source. Mineral values (calcium, magnesium, sodium) are consistent with the official San Pellegrino product label and USDA Branded Foods data.
notes: Zero-macro justification: energy_kcal is naturally zero as this is pure mineral water containing no caloric nutrients. protein_g is naturally zero — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. fat_g is naturally zero — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. sugar_g is naturally zero. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, dietary_fibre_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero for this product. Sentinel value (-1) fields: potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA Branded Foods entry for this product. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are all -1 as they are not reported in the USDA Branded Foods entry for this mineral water product. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving is 0.0 since carbohydrate_g is 0. Multilingual local_names are null as this is an Italian commercial product (not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish) and no mandatory local-language naming rules apply under the Veritas multilingual matrix for Italian cuisine. The romanized Italian product name 'Acqua Minerale San Pellegrino Frizzante' is captured in romanized_local and aliases. Calcium value of 53 mg/100 ml and magnesium value of 24 mg/100 ml are consistent with the official San Pellegrino product specification and USDA Branded Foods data. Sodium of 10.8 mg/100 ml is consistent with the product label. Price data from field survey data (SGD 7.08) is preserved in source context but not included in the nutrient record as it is platform-specific and not a nutritional field.

improvement_actions_taken: Applied zero-macro justification rule: documented 'naturally zero' and 'confirmed zero' for all zero core macros in notes field.
Applied zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule: set glycemic_index to 1 (not null or 0), gi_category to 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving to 0.0, documented in notes and gi_source.
Applied sentinel values documentation rule: documented all -1 fields (potassium_mg, iron_mg, and all vitamins/trace minerals) in notes with explicit source justification.
Applied ontology match_type rule: used only 'exact', 'closest_parent', or 'none' for all three ontology match_type fields.
Applied aliases consistency rule: verified all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set GI category to 'low' consistent with GI=1 (≤55 threshold rule).
Populated nutrient_source with USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods as approved source for commercial packaged products.
Populated health_context with all three required conditions including Type 2 Diabetes with correct human_condition_name and machine key.
